text: The surgeon marks a diamond shape on the abdominal wall, maintaining an 18-centimeter distance from the target site. A stab wound is made, and a Veress needle is inserted perpendicular to the lifted abdominal wall. The surgeon performs irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm needle placement. Pneumoperitoneum is established with an insufflator, gradually increasing pressure to 15 mmHg and introducing approximately 3.5 liters of gas. The incision is then enlarged to 11 mm.

The surgeon introduces a 10 mm trocar using a screwing motion, ensuring the tip of the index finger is pointed towards the sharp end to prevent injury. A hissing sound is heard as the tubing is attached to the insufflator. The telescope is introduced, and the patient's head is positioned down and right up. The first and second working ports are introduced, allowing for initial diagnostic laparoscopy. The medial, lateral, and median umbilical ligaments are visible, along with the hernia sac.

The surgeon carefully extracts the omentum from the right-sided indirect inguinal hernia sac. Using a 30-degree telescope, the hernia sac is visualized, appearing as a dark cavity. Dissection begins six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the deep ring at the two o'clock position, where the peritoneum is pulled and cut using monopolar scissors or harmonic device, extending medially up to the medial umbilical ligament.

The surgeon begins the lateral dissection to create the preperitoneal pocket using monopolar scissors, a placelet, or a spatula. The dissection occurs over the triangle of doom, ensuring that the triangle of pain is addressed first. The sequence involves lateral dissection followed by medial dissection, with the sac dissection performed last.

The surgeon uses the back of the scissors as a blunt dissector to create a preperitoneal pocket, completing the lateral dissection upon visualizing the spermatic vessel, which marks the lateral boundary of the Triangle of Doom. The left hand supports the peritoneum while the right hand separates it from the transversalis fascia. The medial dissection reveals the inferior epigastric vessel and Cooper's ligament, which is clearly visible. The space of Retzius is enlarged, with the bladder visible below, and the inferior epigastric vessel is seen at the twelve o'clock position.

The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum from the transversalis fascia, ensuring the sac is fully separated without pulling the pseudo sac. One hand pulls the sac while the other pushes the transversalis fascia away. Below, the spermatic vessel and vas deferens are clearly visible. The transversalis fascia is pushed back, and the peritoneum is pulled out, taking care not to injure the vas or spermatic vessel and avoiding any buttonhole formation.

The surgeon uses the left hand to push the transversal fascia back into the defect while the right hand holds the peritoneum. A window is visible between the spermatic vessel and the sac, with the spermatic vessel separated from the sac. The sac is isolated and will be separated from the transversal fascia. The spermatic vessel, also known as the testicular vessel, forms the lateral boundary of the triangle of doom.

The surgeon identifies the pseudo-sac, composed of the transversalis fascia, and the true sac, which is the peritoneum. The peritoneum is being separated from the transversalis fascia, which will be pushed back into the defect by CO₂ pressure. The surgical field shows the triangle of doom with the vas deferens and spermatic vessels clearly visible. The external iliac artery and vein are seen between the iliac vessels. The pocket is enlarged to accommodate the mesh without folding, ensuring proper peritonization.

The surgeon places a mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring it covers the bladder area, Bogros space, trapezoid of disaster, triangle of doom, and triangle of pain. The mesh is spread evenly, with its center aligned to the center of the defect. The inferomedial end of the mesh is fixed to the Cooper's ligament using a tacker. The mesh is then covered by the peritoneum, completing the peritonealization process.
